task: I want to set up a simple maintenance routine for my truck and motorcycle so I’m not just reacting when something goes wrong. Can you help me sketch a practical checklist for what to look at on a regular schedule and what should get handled sooner rather than later?

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — a simple routine is a great way to catch small issues before they turn into no-starts, leaks, or expensive repairs.

Here’s a practical, shop-style checklist you can use for both the truck and the motorcycle.

---

## 1) Every time you use it
Quick walk-around, 2–5 minutes.

### Truck
- Check for any new fluid spots under it
- Look at tires for:
  - low pressure
  - uneven wear
  - cuts, bulges, nails
- Confirm lights work:
  - headlights
  - brake lights
  - turn signals
  - reverse lights
- Listen for:
  - squealing belts
  - grinding brakes
  - clicking, clunking, or new vibration
- Check dash for warning lights

### Motorcycle
- Look for leaks under the bike
- Check tire pressure and tire condition
- Verify:
  - headlight
  - brake light
  - turn signals
  - horn
- Squeeze front brake and step on rear brake to feel for normal resistance
- Check chain condition if it has one:
  - slack
  - dryness
  - rust
- Make sure mirrors and controls are normal

---

## 2) Weekly or every couple of fuel fill-ups
Good “keep it honest” check.

### Truck
- Engine oil level
- Coolant level in reservoir
- Washer fluid
- Tire pressures
- Quick look at battery terminals:
  - corrosion
  - looseness
- Check for fresh leaks around:
  - engine
  - transmission
  - power steering
  - axle/differential

### Motorcycle
- Engine oil level
- Coolant level if liquid-cooled
- Chain slack/lube if applicable
- Tire pressures
- Brake fluid level
- Check battery connections if accessible
- Look over cables and hoses for rubbing or cracking

---

## 3) Monthly
A little more detailed.

### Truck
- Inspect battery terminals and grounds closely
- Look at belts and hoses for cracking, swelling, or glazing
- Check air filter condition
- Look at brake pad life if visible
- Inspect suspension/steering for:
  - worn bushings
  - torn boots
  - loose parts
- Check windshield wipers and washer spray

### Motorcycle
- Clean and lube chain if needed
- Inspect brake pads and rotors
- Check fork seals for oil
- Inspect tires for age and wear pattern
- Check fasteners that tend to loosen from vibration
- Look over clutch/brake lever feel and cable condition if applicable

---

## 4) Every oil change / major service interval
Use the manufacturer’s schedule as the baseline.

### Truck
- Change engine oil and filter
- Rotate tires if applicable
- Inspect:
  - brakes
  - suspension
  - steering
  - belts
  - hoses
  - battery
- Check all fluid levels and condition
- Replace air filter if dirty
- Inspect cabin filter if equipped

### Motorcycle
- Change engine oil and filter
- Check valve adjustment interval if your bike has one
- Inspect chain and sprockets
- Check brake fluid condition
- Inspect coolant condition
- Check tires for age and tread
- Inspect wheel bearings and steering head feel if you notice looseness

---

## 5) Handle sooner rather than later
These are “don’t wait” items.

### Stop and inspect now if you notice:
- New warning light that stays on
- Battery going weak or slow cranking
- Repeated blown fuses
- Brake pedal or lever feel changing
- A fluid leak that is growing
- Overheating
- Strong fuel smell
- Grinding brakes
- Steering pulling hard or wandering
- Tire bulge, sidewall crack, or exposed cord
- Chain with tight spots, heavy rust, or excessive slack
- Any new vibration, shake, or clunk that wasn’t there before

### Get it checked sooner if:
- Oil level drops between services
- Coolant level keeps dropping
- Battery needs frequent charging
- Tires wear unevenly
- Brakes squeal, pulse, or feel soft
- The bike starts harder than usual
- The truck has intermittent no-starts or electrical weirdness

---

## 6) Simple rule of thumb
If it affects:
- **braking**
- **steering**
- **tires**
- **charging/electrical**
- **cooling**
- **oil level**

…it should move to the top of the list.

---

## 7) Easy way to keep it practical
Use a small note in your phone with:
- date
- mileage
- tire pressures
- oil level
- battery issues
- any leaks
- anything “not normal”

That way you can spot patterns instead of guessing.

If you want, I can turn this into:
1. a **one-page printable checklist**, or  
2. a **truck vs motorcycle maintenance schedule by mileage/months**.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah, that works. Straightforward and usable. The “don’t wait” list is the part I’d want to keep at the top.

I’d probably actually use it if it were tightened into a one-page checklist by interval — daily, weekly, monthly, service interval — with a separate red-flag section for safety items. That format fits how I keep shop procedures and it’d be easier to stick on the wall or keep in the cab.
